query: hemisection vs mandibular root resection techniques and terminology

retrieved_docs.title: Hemifacial Microsomia -- Staging -- Pruzansky Classification
retrieved_docs.content: This system categorizes mandibular hypoplasia into 3 groups based on radiological features, later modified by Kaban et al to incorporate TMJ status. Grade 1: Mandible smaller than the unaffected side Grade 2a: Shortened ramus with a normal glenoid fossa Grade 2b: Shortened ramus with malpositioned glenoid fossa requiring TMJ reconstruction Grade 3: Severe distortion or absence (agenesis) of the ramus [60]

retrieved_docs.title: Glossectomy -- Technique or Treatment -- Lip-Split Mandibulotomy Glossectomy Approach
retrieved_docs.content: Gingival cuts are made at this point in the procedure. Management of the central incisors is at the surgeon's discretion. If not extracted, the roots are at risk of exposure or being dislodged from their periodontal ligament during the sagittal osteotomy. A 15-blade is best for making the gingival cuts between the central incisors as it preserves the most mucosa and does not transmit unintended heat damage that could occur with monopolar cautery. Especially in radiated tissue, monopolar cautery use at this step frequently ablates the gingival tissue, leaving a tissue gap and increasing the risk of a salivary fistula.

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Osteoradionecrosis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The evaluation should include: Review radiotherapy and dental record Biopsy of wound Panographic Radiographs CT of mandible Stage the disease Rule out recurrence/persistence of primary malignancy or new secondary malignancy by getting a biopsy of the wound. [9] [10] [11] [8] Imaging studies aid in the diagnosis and quantify the extent of disease. CT findings: Cortical bone disruption with mixed lysis and sclerosis can be seen. Pathologic fractures can also be seen. Panographic radiograph findings: Plain radiographs are useful for screening but can underestimate the extent of lesions. Lytic areas with ill-defined, non-sclerotic borders can be seen. Gather record of radiotherapy and review treatment method, radiation portal, and total dose. Marx Staging of ORN is the most common staging system in use. It is based on response to hyperbaric oxygen therapy (HBO).

retrieved_docs.title: Osteoradionecrosis(Archived) -- Treatment / Management -- Scale of Osteoradionecrosis
retrieved_docs.content: Stage II: These are patients who do not respond favorably to 30 pre-operative treatments, or when a more major operative debridement is required. Surgery for stage II osteoradionecrosis patients must be focused on preserving the integrity of the mandible. If mandibular resection is anticipated, patients are advanced to Stage III.

retrieved_docs.title: Transgender Surgery of the Head and Neck -- Technique or Treatment -- Genioplasty
retrieved_docs.content: The mental nerves are typically identified at this stage for their protection during the remainder of the procedure. Using electrocautery or a reciprocating saw, the midline of the mandible should be marked from the level of the incision down to the inferior margin to ensure that the osteotomized segment can be realigned properly at the conclusion of the procedure. The saw is then used to make a transverse osteotomy through the anterior mandible, taking care to remain inferior to the course of the inferior alveolar nerves, which may dip below the level of the mental foramina; as with mandibular angle reduction, preoperative CT imaging will allow the surgeon to plan safe osteotomies.

retrieved_docs.title: Glossectomy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Glossectomy refers to a group of surgical procedures that involve the resection of a portion or the entirety of the tongue. Although there are various ways to classify glossectomy, it is commonly categorized based on laterality (left, right, or midline) and the amount of tongue removed. The classifications include: Partial glossectomy : removal of less than half of the tongue Hemiglossectomy : removal of half of the tongue Subtotal glossectomy : removal of more than half but less than the entire tongue Total glossectomy : complete excision of the tongue

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Inferior Alveolar Nerve
retrieved_docs.content: The Rood classification is a valuable assessment tool that uses radiographs to predict IAN injury in 3rd molar extractions. [8] Several radiographic features on panoramic radiographs suggest proximity to the IAN, and the presence of 1 or more of these signs warrants a more complex imaging examination by CBCT. Signs to watch for include the loss of cortication of the inferior dental canal (IDC), seen as an interruption of the radiopaque line that lies above and below the IAN canal narrowing, alongside deviation of the IDC, darkening or narrowing of the roots, and dilaceration of the roots (see Image. Radiographic Features Suggestive of Close Proximity to the Inferior Dental Canal). [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Osteoradionecrosis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Stage 1 Exposed alveolar bone without pathologic fracture, which responds to hyperbaric oxygen therapy and minor bony debridement Stage 2 The disease does not respond to 30 daily HBO treatments with minor bony debridement, or it requires major bony debridement initially. They are then considered Stage 2 and receive more radical surgical debridement plus ten postoperative HBO treatments. Stage 3 Failed treatment in stage 1 or 2 or initially present with signs below: Pathologic fracture Orocutaneous fistulae Evidence of lytic involvement of inferior mandibular border Treatment involves mandibular segmental resection of all necrotic bone and 30 preoperative and ten postoperative HBO treatments.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Radiographic examination is particularly important when dealing with retained roots. With the increasing availability and popularity of cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT), it has become much easier to determine the specific depth, position, size, curvature, and proximity of remaining root fragments to nearby anatomical landmarks like the maxillary sinus and the inferior alveolar nerve. [14] This is the primary limitation of traditional two-dimensional radiographs, though they are still indicated in the initial assessment of retained roots. Not all exodontia cases require a CBCT evaluation, but it becomes valuable when the anticipated surgery increases the risk of iatrogenic damage. Pre-operative evaluation not only gives the surgeon the means to generate an appropriate diagnosis but also determines the proper course of treatment. [14] The surgeon must exercise sound clinical judgment and base their decision-making on empirical evidence and best practices when treating retained root tips. Lastly, the surgeon must decide if they have the clinical skills and armamentarium necessary to perform the procedure safely and effectively. It is never the wrong answer to refer a patient to a higher level of care or to a provider with known expertise in treating a particular condition. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemifacial Microsomia -- Treatment / Management -- Mandibular Distraction Osteogenesis
retrieved_docs.content: McCarthy popularized mandibular distraction osteogenesis (MDO) in the 1990s. [43] The procedure expands the mandible by lengthening the bone itself. Bilateral mandibular osteotomies create segments gradually drawn apart to stimulate new bone growth between them. Unlike graft placement, MDO depends on the formation of new bone rather than donor material. Advantages of MDO over grafts include lower recurrence of asymmetry, less blood loss, shorter operative time, improved soft tissue symmetry, absence of donor site morbidity, and suitability for younger patients. [44] [45] [46]

retrieved_docs.content: Lastly, osteomyelitis, osteoradionecrosis, and medication-related osteonecrosis of the jaws (MRONJ) warrant special consideration due to the extent of the necessary evaluation, which is beyond the scope of this activity. [40] [41] [42] [43] [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Hemifacial Microsomia -- Staging -- Orbit, Mandible, Ear, Nerve, Soft Tissue Classification
retrieved_docs.content: The OMENS system provides a more comprehensive assessment, grading 5 anatomical categories: orbit, mandible, ear, nerve, and soft tissue. Each category is scored from 0 (normal) to 3 (most severe). The OMENS plus system further incorporates extracranial abnormalities. This classification is considered flexible and sensitive, effectively capturing the broad phenotypic spectrum of HFM.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Closed Surgical Technique: This technique involves elevating, luxating, and delivering the root segment from the coronal aspect of the socket without creating a mucoperiosteal flap. Once appropriate surgical visualization of the root segment is achieved, dental elevators can be used in succession using one of three fundamental physics concepts: (1) a wheel, (2) a lever, and (3) a wedge. When using an elevator as a wheel, a rotational arc between the elevator tip and the body of the root develops to lift the root out coronally. Cryer elevators perform well when used in this manner. When using an elevator as a class 1 lever, a fulcrum point is created between the alveolar crest and the root. This concept requires a reliable purchase point so that force can be applied without instrument slippage or misdirection. [14] If the current purchase point is inadequate, it may be necessary to create a bony trough using a surgical handpiece or osteotome adjacent to the root. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Transgender Surgery of the Head and Neck -- Technique or Treatment -- Mandibular Angle Reduction
retrieved_docs.content: The cervical branch of the facial nerve lies inferior to the marginal mandibular branch. It is consistently found 1 cm below the halfway point of a line drawn between the mentum and the mastoid tip, although proximally in its course, it may share a common root with the marginal mandibular branch. [38] A nerve stimulator will help identify motor nerve branches and may prevent iatrogenic injuries, provided the patient has not received paralytic drugs. Once the masseter has been exposed safely, it can be incised along the posterior border of the mandibular angle and elevated off the bone. A reciprocating saw is then used to perform the osteotomy. The medial pterygoid muscle is elevated from the medial surface of the bone fragment to liberate it completely.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: With the invention of the rotary surgical drill, the transition from simple to surgical exodontia has never been easier. Some say surgical drills are one of the greatest innovations in modern exodontia. [14] They minimize operating time and allow the surgeon to access points of the tooth that were previously inaccessible through simple exodontia methods. [14] When indicated, cortical bone can be drilled away, or roots can be sectioned to increase purchase points and allow for a more profound apical orientation of elevators. When multi-rooted teeth require sectioning with a drill, it is advantageous to treat each root as a single-rooted tooth when extracting, as it promotes less trauma to the supporting alveolus. [21] Maxillary molar roots are traditionally sectioned at the furcation level in a Y or T configuration. [20] In contrast, mandibular molar roots and other 2-rooted teeth are sectioned horizontally through their respective furcations. [22]

Within the procedural taxonomy, Excision serves as a parent category for “Clinical actions A – G,” indicating that these clinical actions are sub‑categories of the broader excision concept. At the same time, certain spinal procedures are explicitly excluded from the Excision grouping; these include laminectomy with exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina (without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy) for one or two vertebral segments in the cervical, thoracic, or sacral regions, as well as multisegment laminectomy (> 2 vertebral segments) in the cervical and thoracic regions. This delineation clarifies that such laminectomy techniques are not considered forms of Excision. Excision is also linked to procedures involving the head and neck region. retrieved_docs.content: (posterior fossa) procedures, suboccipital craniectomy for cranial nerve decompression, and cervical laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and disc excision. Endocrine neck surgery also employs excision for parathyroidectomy or exploration of the parathyroids, including variants that involve mediastinal exploration, sternal split, or transthoracic approaches. Excision is explicitly excluded as a method for certain extensive spinal procedures: laminectomy with exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy that spans more than two thoracic vertebral segments is not considered an excision technique. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1502
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is classified as a clinical action within the broader group “Clinical actions A‑G” and serves as an umbrella term for a wide range of operative procedures. It includes adrenalectomy—whether partial or complete, performed transabdominally, laparoscopically, or dorsally, with or without biopsy and with possible removal of adjacent retroperitoneal tumor—as well as several biliary surgeries such as cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct, choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, and associated cholangiography, which can be performed via open or laparoscopic techniques. Craniectomy or craniotomy performed exploratively in the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region also falls under Excision, as do various parathyroidectomy approaches—including cervical, sternal‑split, and transthoracic routes—each potentially combined with mediastinal exploration. In spinal surgery, Excision encompasses laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and excision of herniated intervertebral disc at a single lumbar level, but explicitly excludes laminectomy procedures that involve only exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy for spinal stenosis across cervical and thoracic segments (both 1‑2 vertebral levels and more extensive segments). Additionally, Excision covers “other or therapeutic procedures on nose, mouth and pharynx” and is linked to plastic procedures on the nose, highlighting its relevance to both general surgical and specialized reconstructive interventions. This comprehensive definition clarifies the scope of Excision, distinguishing it from specific laminectomy techniques while integrating diverse surgical modalities under a single procedural category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1619
retrieved_docs.content: exploration, sometimes requiring a sternal split or transthoracic approach. In addition, Excision is linked to other therapeutic interventions on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, indicating that it also serves as a methodological category for certain plastic or reconstructive surgeries in the facial region. Conversely, several laminectomy techniques—specifically decompressive laminectomy of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy—are explicitly excluded from the Excision category. This exclusion applies to cervical, lumbar (except spondylolisthesis), sacral, and thoracic laminectomies involving one or two vertebral segments, as well as cervical laminectomies extending over more than two vertebral segments. Thus, Excision functions as an umbrella term for diverse operative procedures ranging from endocrine gland removal and biliary duct exploration to cranial and urogenital tumor resections, while clearly distinguishing itself from certain spinal

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2756
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is presented as a broad surgical category that includes a wide variety of organ‑specific and region‑specific procedures while explicitly excluding certain extensive spinal decompressions. Within this category, adrenalectomy—whether partial or complete, performed transabdominally, lumbarly, or dorsally, and sometimes accompanied by removal of an adjacent retroperitoneal tumor—uses an excision method. Cholecystectomy combined with exploration of the common bile duct, either with cholangiography or with choledochoenterostomy, is also classified as an excision technique. Exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region falls under the same umbrella. In the spine, excision encompasses laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and removal of herniated intervertebral disc when performed at a single interspace in the cervical or lumbar spine. By contrast, more extensive laminectomy procedures that decompress the spinal cord or cauda equina over more than two vertebral segments—whether cervical, lumbar, or thoracic—are specifically listed as excluded methods for excision. Other procedures grouped under excision include nipple exploration with lactiferous‑duct excision, parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration, and parathyroidectomy that involves mediastinal exploration via a sternal split approach. Excision is positioned within the higher‑level grouping “Clinical actions A‑G,” indicating that it is one of the clinical actions encompassed by that classification. Additionally, excision is linked to plastic procedures on the nose and is identified as a method within the broader set of “other or therapeutic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx,” reflecting its relevance to reconstructive and therapeutic interventions in those anatomical regions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 675
retrieved_docs.content: Other OR therapeutic procedures on bone is the classification category that includes the surgical intervention known as total ostectomy of tarsals and metatarsals.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1894
retrieved_docs.content: Complete excision is a broad surgical concept that encompasses the specific procedure استئصال العقد اللمفاوية الجذري, which involves the removal of root lymph nodes. This radical lymph‑node excision is classified as a child of the category Other therapeutic procedures; hemic and lymphatic system, indicating that it is one of the therapeutic interventions applied to the hemic and lymphatic system. Recognizing استئصال العقد اللمفاوية الجذري as a type of complete excision within this therapeutic group helps clinicians understand its role in the comprehensive surgical management of lymphatic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2570
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is a broad surgical category that includes a wide range of operative techniques across several organ systems. It encompasses adrenalectomy procedures—both partial and complete, or exploration of the adrenal gland with or without biopsy performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches, including cases with excision of an adjacent retroperitoneal tumor—as well as choledochotomy or choledochostomy with exploration, drainage, or calculus removal, which may be combined with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty. Excision also covers exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the supratentorial region, and various laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) techniques for cervical and lumbar nerve‑root decompression that may involve partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and excision of herniated intervertebral disc. Laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration is another method classified under Excision, along with multiple forms of parathyroidectomy—standard cervical parathyroidectomy, parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration via cervical, sternal split, or transthoracic approaches, and parathyroidectomy combined with mediastinal exploration. Excision is further linked to “other or therapeutic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx” and is associated with plastic procedures on the nose, reflecting its inclusion in the broader grouping “Clinical actions A‑G.” However, certain spinal procedures are explicitly excluded from the Excision category, notably laminectomy with exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy (e.g., procedures for spinal stenosis involving one or two vertebral segments). This integrated overview delineates the diverse methods that fall under Excision, clarifies which procedures are excluded, and highlights its relevance for procedural classification and patient‑care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1712
retrieved_docs.content: Excision serves as a broad parent category for a variety of surgical actions, including the set of procedures labeled “Clinical actions A‑G.” Within this overarching category, many specific operative methods are classified as forms of excision: cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration (both open and laparoscopic), choledochotomy or choledochostomy with duct exploration, drainage or calculus removal (with or without cholecystotomy and with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty), transcranial orbital exploration with lesion removal, cervical and lumbar laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) that decompress nerve roots and may involve partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, or disc excision, radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration, and parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration performed via a cervical approach or a sternal split approach. At the same time, the excision category explicitly excludes several laminectomy techniques used for spinal stenosis: thoracic laminectomy with cord or cauda equina decompression without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy for one or two vertebral segments; and cervical, lumbar, or thoracic laminectomy for more than two vertebral segments performed under the same constraints. Excision is also linked to other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it has a relationship with plastic procedures on the nose, indicating that excisional techniques extend beyond deep organ surgery to include facial and upper aerodigestive tract interventions. This integrated view highlights excision as a versatile surgical concept that encompasses numerous specific operative methods while delineating clear boundaries for procedures it does not cover.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1752
retrieved_docs.content: Complete excision is a broad surgical principle that entails the total removal of targeted tissue, and the Arabic term استئصال العقد اللمفاوية الجذري represents a specific application of this principle in which the root lymph nodes are entirely removed. This relationship highlights that radical lymph node excision (استئصال العقد اللمفاوية الجذري) is a particular form of complete excision, illustrating how general surgical concepts are operationalized in specialized procedures.
